messages.content:
Using someone else's information without mentioning the source, is not plagiarism if ...

 

		
the data consists of numerical material that can be consulted by anyone.


		
this information is considered to be general knowledge.


		
the data refer to figures or images.


		
you use exactly the same words of the original author

Using someone else's information without mentioning the source is not plagiarism if the data consist of numerical material that can be consulted by anyone, if the information is considered to be general knowledge, if the data refer to figures or images, or if you use exactly the same words of the original author.

Using someone else's information without mentioning the source, is not plagiarism if ...

 

		
the data consists of numerical material that can be consulted by anyone.


		
this information is considered to be general knowledge.


		
the data refer to figures or images.


		
you use exactly the same words of the original author

Using someone else's information without mentioning the source is not plagiarism if the data consist of numerical material that can be consulted by anyone, if the information is considered to be general knowledge, if the data refer to figures or images, or if you use exactly the same words of the original author.

Using someone else's information without mentioning the source, is not plagiarism if ...

 

		
the data consists of numerical material that can be consulted by anyone.


		
this information is considered to be general knowledge.


		
the data refer to figures or images.


		
you use exactly the same words of the original author.  Which one is true？

Apologies for the confusion. Using someone else's information without mentioning the source is not plagiarism if you use exactly the same words of the original author, but it is still considered good practice to cite your sources. However, for the other scenarios - if the data consists of numerical material that can be consulted by anyone, if the information is considered to be general knowledge, or if the data refer to figures or images - it is still advisable to provide proper attribution to the original source to avoid potential plagiarism issues.

You write a piece of text in which you explain a concept that you came up with yourself. But you make intensive use of pieces of text and sentences that you came across while reading articles. Because the concept you describe is yours, you do not have to mention the words you have used.

 

		
That is not true, because you copy someone else's text without mentioning the source.


		
That is right, because the concept is important, not the words.


		
That is right, if you have copied text from sources other than academic articles.


		
That is right, if you have copied text from academic articles.

That is not true, because you copy someone else's text without mentioning the source. Even if the concept is original, using the words and sentences of others without proper citation can still be considered plagiarism. It is important to give credit to the original sources to avoid any ethical or legal issues.

In a text written by a student it says:

Decision-making is of course central in organizations and artificial intelligent (AI) algorithms are increasingly being used in organizational decision-making. As Pasquale points out, "authority is increasingly expressed in algorithms" (2015: 8). This development is welcomed by some and criticised by others; the popular debate is whether or not algorithms will become more 'intelligent' than humans and degrade them to insignificance (Barrat, 2013), or turn out to be a blessing for mankind and management (Amabile, 2019; Bostrom, 2014).

The original text states:

Decision-making is, of course, central to organizations, and artificial intelligent (AI) algorithms are increasingly being used in organizational decision-making. As Pasquale notes, “authority is increasingly expressed algorithmically” (2015: 8). This development is applauded by some and criticized by others; the popular debate is whether or not algorithms will become ‘more intelligent’ than humans and relegate them to insignificance (Barrat, 2013), or turn out to be a blessing for humanity and management (Amabile, 2019; Bostrom, 2014).

The student's text is...


		
no plagiarism because there is a correct reference to the sources used, but it is an unacceptable working method.



		
plagiarism because the student’s text is almost exactly the same as the original text.



		
plagiarism because not only is there no reference data included about the original text, but also because the student’s text is almost an exact copy of the original text with the exception of a few changed words.

		
no plagiarism because there is a correct reference to the sources used.

The student's text is plagiarism because not only is there no reference data included about the original text, but the student's text is almost an exact copy of the original text with the exception of a few changed words.

In an article you read that the outdated Economic Competition Act dates from 1956. You did not know this before. Since you have read other articles about the Economic Competition Act, you have come across that date every time, so you know that fact by now. You give this information in your paper without mentioning the source.

 

		
This is all right, because you have acquired this knowledge in the meantime.


		
This is not okay, because that law is not recent enough.


		
This is not okay, because it is plagiarism.


		
This is all right.

This is not okay because it is plagiarism. Even if you have come across the information multiple times in your readings, failing to mention the source of the information is still considered plagiarism. It is important to provide proper citation to give credit to the original source.

In the reference list, the information you give about a source you found on the internet contains ...


		
information concerning a published book with a similar content.

		
information concerning a published academic article with a similar content.

		
nothing, since you do not have to refer to this source.

		
information concerning the web address of this source and the date of consultation.

The correct answer is: information concerning the web address of this source and the date of consultation. When referencing a source found on the internet, it is important to include the web address (URL) of the source and the date on which you accessed the source. This helps in providing accurate information and allows others to access the same source if needed.

If you refer in the same sentence, in parentheses, to several publications by different authors, the ranking of those sources will take place:


		
alphabetically by name of the author(s). The different sources are separated by a comma.

		
chronologically on publication date. The different sources are separated by a comma.

		
chronologically on publication date. The different sources are separated by a semicolon.

		
alphabetically by name of the author(s). The different sources are separated by a semicolon.

The correct answer is: alphabetically by name of the author(s). The different sources are separated by a semicolon. When referring to multiple sources in parentheses, the sources should be listed alphabetically by the name of the author(s) and separated by semicolons.

The complete reference of a source is: Ashton, R. H., Graul, P. R., & Newton, P. R. (1989). Audit delay and the timeliness of corporate reporting. Contemporary Accounting Research, 5(2), 657-673.

Which statement is correct:


		
All references to this source should be: (Ashton, Graul & Newton, 1989).

		
When you first refer to this source, you use: (Ashton, Graul & Newton, 1989). As from the second reference to this source you use: (Ashton et al., 1989).

		
All references to this source should be: (Ashton et al., 1989).

		
A first reference to this source is: (Ashton et al., 1989). As from the second reference to this source you use: (Ashton, Graul & Newton, 1989).

The correct statement is: A first reference to this source is: (Ashton et al., 1989). As from the second reference to this source, you use: (Ashton, Graul & Newton, 1989).

When referencing a source for the first time, it is common to list all authors (up to three) followed by the publication year. Subsequent references to the same source can use "et al." to indicate multiple authors, while still including the first author's name.

Is this true? ‘It is better to mention too many sources in your text than too few’.


		
Yes, because you should mention each source more than once to show that it is a useful source.

		
No.

		
Yes, because you have to mention as many sources as possible to enhance the academic feel of your text.

		
Yes, because a source too many may be a bit excessive, but in any case preferable to committing plagiarism because you failed to mention a source.

Yes, because a source too many may be a bit excessive, but in any case preferable to committing plagiarism because you failed to mention a source. It is better to err on the side of caution and provide proper attribution to all sources that have contributed to your work. This not only avoids plagiarism but also enhances the credibility and academic integrity of your text.

Is this true? ‘It is better to mention too many sources in your text than too few’.


		
Yes, because you should mention each source more than once to show that it is a useful source.

		
No.

		
Yes, because you have to mention as many sources as possible to enhance the academic feel of your text.

		
Yes, because a source too many may be a bit excessive, but in any case preferable to committing plagiarism because you failed to mention a source.

No. The correct answer is: No. It is not better to mention too many sources in your text than too few. While it is important to appropriately cite and reference the sources that have influenced your work, it is equally important to maintain a balance. Including excessive or unrelated sources may clutter your text and dilute the clarity of your arguments. It is important to cite and reference the sources that are relevant and directly support your work.

Is this true? ‘It is better to mention too many sources in your text than too few’.


		
Yes, because you should mention each source more than once to show that it is a useful source.

		
No.

		
Yes, because you have to mention as many sources as possible to enhance the academic feel of your text.

		
Yes, because a source too many may be a bit excessive, but in any case preferable to committing plagiarism because you failed to mention a source.

The correct answer is: No. It is not better to mention too many sources in your text than too few. Including excessive sources can make the text cluttered and impede the clarity of your message. It is important to include relevant and influential sources that support your arguments and provide credibility to your work. Quality and relevance are more important than quantity when it comes to citing sources.

If you refer to information from different sources in a sentence and you mention the source data in parentheses, you should ...



		
list all references in random order.

		
limit information to only one source, because you may only refer to a maximum of one reference per sentence.

		
list the references in chronological order.

		
list the references in alphabetical order.

The correct answer is: list the references in alphabetical order. When referring to information from different sources in a sentence and providing the source data in parentheses, it is customary to list the references in alphabetical order by the last name of the author(s). This helps in structuring the references and allows for easier identification of the sources.

You want to quote from a PhD thesis that was successfully defended in the past.

Which statement is correct?


		
This is allowed, but not necessary.

		
This is allowed on the condition that you correctly refer to it.

		
This is allowed on the condition that you only mention that quote and do not reveal the actual thesis.

		
This is never allowed.

The correct statement is: This is allowed on the condition that you correctly refer to it. Quoting from a successfully defended PhD thesis is permitted in academic writing, as long as you provide proper citation and attribution to the source. It is important to accurately refer to the thesis and include all necessary information to locate and identify it.

Taking note of some of the ideas from an article and then forgetting to refer to the article when creating your own paper, is considered to be ...


		
no plagiarism, but only if you can demonstrate by means of an otherwise plagiarism-free paper that it really was a matter of oblivion.



		
an acceptable method of working.



		
plagiarism.



		
no plagiarism, but it is an unacceptable way of working.

Taking note of some ideas from an article and then forgetting to refer to the article when creating your own paper is considered to be plagiarism. It is important to provide proper attribution and citation to all sources from which you have derived ideas or information. Forgetting to reference a source does not excuse the act of plagiarism.

To base your entire paper on the ideas and findings of one article, in which you do refer to this article, is considered to be ...


		
an acceptable working method if you did not find another article on the subject via the search functions of the University Library website.



		
plagiarism.



		
no plagiarism, but it is an unacceptable way of working.



		
an acceptable method of working.

Basing your entire paper on the ideas and findings of one article, even if you refer to that article, is considered to be an unacceptable way of working. It is important to consult multiple sources and provide a range of perspectives and evidence in your paper to ensure an informed and comprehensive analysis. Relying solely on one article can limit the depth and breadth of your research and can be seen as a lack of critical engagement with the topic.

What is not a decisive reason to use references?


		
You have use references because this is how you show whether or not you master the subject.



		
You have use references to prove your intellectual honesty.



		
You must use references in order for your text to be verifiable by third parties.



		
You have to use references because in that way you prove you have mastered the reference style.

The statement "You have to use references because in that way you prove you have mastered the reference style" is not a decisive reason to use references. While it is important to properly follow the reference style guidelines, the main purpose of using references is to support your arguments, provide evidence, and give credit to the original sources of information. Demonstrating mastery of the reference style is secondary to the primary purpose of accurately and ethically citing sources.

You write your text in English but you want to use information from a Dutch text.

If you want to quote, you need to do this in …

 

		
Dutch, because quoting is literally and needs to be in the language in which the source is written.



		
English, because quoting needs to be in the language in which you write your text.



		
English, because quoting is describing very precisely what is meant.



		
English, because quoting is literally thus you translate every word correctly.

The correct answer is: English, because quoting needs to be in the language in which you write your text. When incorporating information from a Dutch text into your English text, you should translate the quote into English and provide an accurate representation of the original meaning. Quoting in the same language as your text ensures consistency and comprehensibility for your readers.

In the reference list, the information you give about a source you found on the internet contains ...


		
information concerning a published book with a similar content.

		
information concerning a published academic article with a similar content.

		
nothing, since you do not have to refer to this source.

		
information concerning the web address of this source and the date of consultation.

The correct answer is: information concerning the web address of this source and the date of consultation. In the reference list, when referencing a source found on the internet, it is important to include the web address (URL) of the source and the date on which you accessed or consulted the source. This helps in providing accurate information and allows others to access the same source if needed.

If you refer in the same sentence, in parentheses, to several publications by different authors, the ranking of those sources will take place:


		
alphabetically by name of the author(s). The different sources are separated by a comma.

		
chronologically on publication date. The different sources are separated by a comma.

		
chronologically on publication date. The different sources are separated by a semicolon.

		
alphabetically by name of the author(s). The different sources are separated by a semicolon.

The ranking of those sources will take place: alphabetically by name of the author(s). The different sources are separated by a semicolon. When referring to multiple sources in parentheses within the same sentence, it is customary to list the sources in alphabetical order of the authors' last names, and separate them with semicolons. This helps in organizing the references and maintaining clarity in the citation.

The complete reference of a source is: Ashton, R. H., Graul, P. R., & Newton, P. R. (1989). Audit delay and the timeliness of corporate reporting. Contemporary Accounting Research, 5(2), 657-673.

Which statement is correct:


		
All references to this source should be: (Ashton, Graul & Newton, 1989).

		
When you first refer to this source, you use: (Ashton, Graul & Newton, 1989). As from the second reference to this source you use: (Ashton et al., 1989).

		
All references to this source should be: (Ashton et al., 1989).

		
A first reference to this source is: (Ashton et al., 1989). As from the second reference to this source you use: (Ashton, Graul & Newton, 1989).

The correct statement is: When you first refer to this source, you use: (Ashton, Graul & Newton, 1989). As from the second reference to this source, you use: (Ashton et al., 1989).

When referencing a source for the first time, it is common to list all authors (up to three) followed by the publication year. However, for subsequent references to the same source, you can use "et al." to indicate multiple authors, while still including the first author's name.

Is this true? ‘It is better to mention too many sources in your text than too few’.


		
Yes, because you should mention each source more than once to show that it is a useful source.

		
No.

		
Yes, because you have to mention as many sources as possible to enhance the academic feel of your text.

		
Yes, because a source too many may be a bit excessive, but in any case preferable to committing plagiarism because you failed to mention a source.
The correct answer is: No. It is not better to mention too many sources in your text than too few. The
